front: What is the formal definition of a partially ordered set (poset)?
back: A partially ordered set is a pair (P, ≤) where P is a set and ≤ is a binary relation on P that is reflexive, antisymmetric, and transitive.

front: List and define the three properties that a relation must satisfy to be a partial order.
back:
1. Reflexive: For all a ∈ P, a ≤ a.
2. Antisymmetric: For all a, b ∈ P, if a ≤ b and b ≤ a, then a = b.
3. Transitive: For all a, b, c ∈ P, if a ≤ b and b ≤ c, then a ≤ c.

front: How does a partially ordered set differ from a totally ordered set?
back: In a poset, not every pair of elements needs to be comparable (i.e., it is possible that for some a, b ∈ P, neither a ≤ b nor b ≤ a holds). In a totally ordered set, every pair of elements is comparable.

front: Provide a common example of a partially ordered set relevant to set theory.
back: The power set of any given set S, denoted by P(S), ordered by the subset relation ⊆. For example, for set S = {1,2}, the poset is (P(S), ⊆) = {∅, {1}, {2}, {1,2}}.

front: What is an upper bound for a subset A of a poset (P, ≤)?
back: An element u ∈ P is an upper bound of A if a ≤ u for every element a ∈ A. Note: u does not necessarily have to be in A itself.
